svg.title: Orbit — Message Send Flow
svg.text: SEQUENCE DIAGRAM — MESSAGE SEND FLOW
React SPA
SockJS
STOMP.js
MessageController
MessageService
Message
Repository
(MongoDB)
Conversation
Repository
(MongoDB)
Notification
Repository
(MongoDB)
Kafka
Producer
Config
Upstash
Kafka
chat.messages
Cloudflare
R2
(file path only)
KafkaConsumer
Recipient Browser
FLOW A — TEXT MESSAGE VIA WEBSOCKET (primary path)
Optimistic UI: message appended to sender's chat instantly, marked "sending…"
before any server response. Reverted on error.
1. STOMP SEND → /app/conversations/{id}/send { type: TEXT, content: "…" }
RateLimitFilter: check 60 msg/min bucket for userId
[rate limit exceeded] → STOMP ERROR frame { status: 429 }
CRITICAL ORDER: MongoDB persist happens FIRST — before any Kafka publish.
If Kafka fails after persist, the message exists in DB and can be recovered. If Kafka were first and MongoDB failed, the message appears in UIs but does not exist.
2. insert message document { type, content, senderId, senderName ✱, conversationId, createdAt }
savedMessage { _id assigned by MongoDB }
3. update conversations.lastMessage snapshot { messageId, content, senderId, senderName, sentAt }
acknowledged
4. upsert notification — increment unreadCount for each participant except sender
acknowledged
5. publish → chat.messages { messageId, conversationId, recipientIds[], messagePayload }
6. event stored on topic partition
ack from Kafka broker
7. STOMP receipt — savedMessage payload (confirmed send)
Optimistic message confirmed: "sending…" replaced with final _id
8. KafkaConsumerConfig on each instance consumes event from chat.messages
check local session registry
push → /topic/conversations/{id}/messages
If recipient is on a DIFFERENT backend instance, the KafkaConsumer on THAT instance delivers it — this is multi-instance fan-out (see Flow D).
If recipient is offline, the event is discarded by all instances. The unread notification count (step 4) is how they learn about missed messages on next login.
✱ senderName is stored as a snapshot at send time, not a live reference — message history is preserved even if the user later changes their display name.
FLOW B — FILE / IMAGE MESSAGE (R2 upload before persist)
Triggered via REST (multipart). WebSocket STOMP cannot carry binary file data.
1. POST /api/v1/conversations/{id}/messages/upload (multipart/form-data)
[invalid type or size] → 400 Bad Request
2. stream file to Cloudflare R2 via R2StorageClient (AWS S3 SDK)
[R2 upload fails] → do NOT persist message → 500 to client
R2 object key + presigned URL (1 hr expiry)
3. insert message { type: IMAGE|FILE, file: { fileId, fileName, mimeType, url } }
savedMessage with _id
Steps 3–8 from Flow A continue identically: update lastMessage → upsert notifications → publish to Kafka → WebSocket fan-out delivery.
R2 upload MUST succeed before message is persisted. DB never holds a message with a broken file reference. Presigned URL expires after 1hr — refresh via GET /api/v1/files/{fileId}/url.
FLOW C — REST FALLBACK (WebSocket unavailable)
Triggered when WebSocket is unavailable — not yet connected, or SockJS is in backoff retry after a disconnect.
1. POST /api/v1/conversations/{id}/messages { type: TEXT, content: "…" } (Bearer token)
Internal flow identical to Flow A steps 2–8: persist → lastMessage → notifications → Kafka publish → WebSocket delivery
2. 201 Created — full message object (same shape as WebSocket receipt)
Client sends an idempotency key (client-generated UUID in X-Idempotency-Key header). Duplicate retries return the already-persisted message, not a duplicate.
FLOW D — MULTI-INSTANCE FAN-OUT (sender and recipient on different backend instances)
Instance A (sender's WebSocket session lives here)
Instance B (recipient's WebSocket session lives here)
1. sender sends message → Instance A handles it (MessageService persists to shared MongoDB Atlas)
2. message persisted (shared MongoDB — accessible by all instances)
savedMessage
3. Instance A publishes event → chat.messages (Upstash Kafka)
4. ALL instances (A, B, others) consume event from their Kafka consumer group
Instance A: no recipient session in registry → discards event silently
Instance B: finds recipient session → pushes via WebSocket
Adding more Render instances requires zero code changes. Kafka consumer group membership handles the routing automatically.
FLOW E — ERROR PATHS
E1 — MongoDB Persist Fails
1. message arrives at MessageService
2. insert message document → MongoDB throws DataAccessException
3. DataAccessException caught — Kafka is NOT published
4. STOMP ERROR frame / 500 JSON → optimistic message reverted → "failed to send" + retry
E2 — Kafka Publish Fails After MongoDB Persist (message exists in DB, real-time delivery failed)
1. publish → chat.messages → Kafka broker unreachable
KafkaProducerException (after 3 retries: 1s → 2s → 4s backoff)
2. sender receives 200/receipt — message was persisted, delivery failed
Message NOT lost — recipients see it on next REST history fetch. This is why persist-before-publish is the correct ordering — the failure state is recoverable.
ORDERING RULES (must be enforced in MessageService without exception)
▸ Persist to MongoDB BEFORE publishing to Kafka — data integrity guarantee
▸ Update lastMessage snapshot AFTER persist, BEFORE Kafka — snapshot must reflect the committed message
▸ Upsert notifications AFTER persist, BEFORE Kafka — offline recipients must have accurate unread counts
